misconceptionSummary: Students think carrying in multiplication is different from carrying in addition.

visuals.ruleText: Carrying in multiplication is regrouping — exactly the same as in addition
visuals.subText: 10 ones regroup to 1 ten; 10 tens regroup to 1 hundred

steps.narration: Carrying in multiplication is the same idea as in addition — regrouping when a column overflows past nine. Ten ones become one ten, which moves to the tens column. The carry records that regrouping.
Expanding shows what the carry represents. Seven times six gives forty — that forty is four tens, which get carried to the tens column. The carry is simply the overflow from the partial product.
Estimate before multiplying. Three hundred and fifty times six is two thousand one hundred — so the answer should be close to that. Two thousand and eighty-two passes the sense check.

visuals.label: Carry = regrouped overflow from partial product

steps.narration: Two thousand and eighty-two. Estimating first, then checking the exact answer is near the estimate, catches most carrying errors.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: treating multiplication carries as an arbitrary rule unconnected to place value. The carry is simply the overflow from a partial product — regrouping, exactly as in column addition.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Carrying in multiplication is regrouping — the same idea as in column addition. When a partial product reaches ten or more, the tens overflow into the next column as a carry.
